did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the ConvNeXt deep learning model for multiclass skin lesion classification using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. It performed a comparative study against other AI models including GoogleNet, DenseNet201, CSLNet, and Swin Transformer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a comparative study of skin lesion classification using the ConvNeXt model on the ISIC 2019 dataset. It includes specific methodology, training details (50 epochs), and quantitative metrics (87.37% Accuracy, 98.08% Specificity), classifying it as a research paper despite being published in a conference proceedings (ICCCNT).

title: Comparative Study of Skin Lesion Classification Using Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2023 14th International Conference on Computing Communication and Networking Technologies (ICCCNT)
brief_summary: This study presents a comparative analysis of skin lesion classification using dermoscopic images to address the challenges of manual diagnosis. The authors employed the ConvNeXt deep learning model on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which contains 25,331 images across eight diagnostic classes. The proposed ConvNeXt model was evaluated against GoogleNet, DenseNet201, CSLNet, and Swin Transformer in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. The ConvNeXt model achieved an accuracy of 87.37%, sensitivity of 89.06%, and specificity of 98.08%. While the Swin Transformer demonstrated higher accuracy (97.2%), the ConvNeXt model exhibited superior specificity compared to the other evaluated models.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset used for multiclass classification. Described as a precancerous condition. 867 images before augmentation, 3468 after.
One of the 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset. Described as a form of cancer primarily emerging on sun-exposed regions. 3323 images.
One of the 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset. Described as not cancerous. 2624 images.
One of the 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset. Described as not cancerous. 239 images before augmentation, 2716 after.
One of the 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset. Described as not cancerous. 12875 images.
One of the 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset. Described as an aggressive form of skin cancer originating in melanocytes. 4522 images.
One of the 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset. Described as developing in squamous cells, usually not life-threatening but aggressive if untreated. 628 images before augmentation, 3175 after.
One of the 8 classes in the ISIC 2019 dataset. Described as potentially cancerous or non-cancerous. 253 images before augmentation, 2848 after.

all_task_types.target: Multiclass skin lesion diagnostic categories (Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic nevus, Melanoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into 8 distinct diagnostic categories using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete class labels rather than continuous values. Performance metrics include Accuracy (87.37%), Sensitivity (89.06%), Specificity (98.08%), Precision, Recall, and F1-Score per class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing images of various skin lesions. The study used the original unbalanced dataset and an augmented version to address class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the ConvNeXt model (80% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation (part of the 20% split, though specific count not explicitly separated from testing in the split description, Table III lists 'Number of Images Tested' which likely corresponds to the test set or validation set used for final metrics).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation (20% of the dataset). The paper reports metrics on this split.
use.other: The original unbalanced dataset (25,331 images) was used as the base before augmentation. The augmented dataset (35,551 images) was used for the final experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using descriptive metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, Recall, F1-Score) and confusion matrices without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC 2019.experiment: Comparative Study of Skin Lesion Classification using ConvNeXt vs Baselines

DenseNet201.Sensitivity: Low (Exact value not reported in Table IV, noted as 'low sensitivity')

ISIC 2019.notes: The ConvNeXt model achieved the highest specificity (98.08%) among all compared models. The Swin Transformer achieved the highest accuracy (97.2%). CSLNet had a slightly higher sensitivity (89.6%) than ConvNeXt (89.06%). DenseNet201 had the lowest accuracy (68.2%). The BKL class had low precision (0.61) and the MEL class had low recall (0.70) and F1-score (0.70) for the proposed ConvNeXt model. DF and VASC classes had high precision, recall, and F1-scores (0.99).

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained ConvNeXt model (specifically derived from a modernized ResNet50 architecture trained with Vision Transformer techniques on ImageNet, as described in the related works section referencing ConvNeXt [10]).

macro_design.depthwise_convolution: 7x7 depthwise convolution in each block, shifted to upper position
macro_design.downsampling: 2x2 convolutional layers with stride of 2 between stages

micro_design.activation_function: GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit), one per block

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, Shear, Zoom, Horizontal Flip, Vertical Flip, Brightness change

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to address class imbalance: Rotation, Shear, Zoom, Horizontal Flip, Vertical Flip, and Brightness change applied to specific classes (AK, DF, SCC, VASC) to balance the dataset.
Resizing: All images resized to 224x224 resolution before training.
Dataset Split: Images split into 80% for training and 20% for testing/validation.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, Shear, Zoom, Horizontal Flip, and Vertical Flip were applied to specific classes (Actinic keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Squamous cell carcinoma, and Vascular lesion) to address class imbalance.
Brightness change was applied to specific classes (Actinic keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Squamous cell carcinoma, and Vascular lesion) to address class imbalance.

Augmentation was selectively applied to underrepresented classes (AK, DF, SCC, VASC) to balance the dataset, increasing the total image count from 25,331 to 35,551. Specific post-augmentation counts: AK (3,468), DF (2,716), SCC (3,175), VASC (2,848). Classes BCC, BKL, NV, and MEL were not augmented.

conclusions: The proposed ConvNeXt model achieved an accuracy of 87.37%, sensitivity of 89.06%, precision of 87.37%, and specificity of 98.08% on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
The ConvNeXt model demonstrated superior specificity (98.08%) compared to the evaluated baseline models (GoogleNet, DenseNet201, CSLNet, and Swin Transformer).
The Swin Transformer model achieved the highest accuracy (97.2%) among the compared models.
CSLNet exhibited a slightly higher sensitivity (89.6%) than the proposed ConvNeXt model (89.06%).
DenseNet201 demonstrated low sensitivity and the lowest accuracy (68.2%) among the compared models.
The ConvNeXt model achieved high precision, recall, and F1-scores (0.99) for the Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular lesion (VASC) classes.
The Benign keratosis (BKL) class had low precision (0.61) for the ConvNeXt model.
The Melanoma (MEL) class had low recall (0.70) and F1-score (0.70) for the ConvNeXt model.
Data augmentation was necessary to address class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset, particularly for underrepresented classes like Dermatofibroma and Vascular lesions.
The study acknowledges that classification accuracy can be improved with more training epochs.
The authors suggest that future work should utilize innovative frameworks to search for better results in skin lesion classification.
important_remarks: The study relies on external benchmark results for comparison models (GoogleNet, DenseNet201, CSLNet, Swin Transformer) rather than retraining them on the same data, which limits the validity of direct performance comparisons due to potential differences in preprocessing, training protocols, and data splits.

The proposed ConvNeXt model achieved an accuracy of 87.37%, which is notably lower than the Swin Transformer's reported accuracy of 97.2% and GoogleNet's 94.9%, suggesting suboptimal performance relative to state-of-the-art baselines in this comparative context.

The model exhibits significant class imbalance issues despite augmentation, with low precision (0.61) for Benign Keratosis (BKL) and low recall/F1-score (0.70) for Melanoma (MEL), indicating poor generalization for critical or ambiguous classes.

The dataset is inherently unbalanced (e.g., NV has 12,875 images vs. DF's 239 originally), and while augmentation was applied to underrepresented classes, the reliance on synthetic augmentation may introduce bias or overfitting risks not fully addressed.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the differences in performance metrics between the proposed model and the baselines.

The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, relying solely on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which limits the assessment of generalizability to diverse clinical populations or different dermoscopic devices.

The paper acknowledges that classification accuracy could be improved with more training epochs, implying the current results (50 epochs) may not represent the model's full potential or optimal convergence.
Deployment concerns are not addressed; the study focuses on offline classification metrics without discussing computational efficiency, inference time, or integration into clinical workflows.
